---
title: "Methods And Architectures"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
---

## Purpose

This page is the glossary of the project. It explains the methods and architectures used in OceanLens, why they
were tested, and which references motivated them.

::: {.method-catalog}
::: {.method-entry}
### CNO

**Role.** Deterministic super-resolution branch. It predicts the stable correction from LR to HR.

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{x}_{LR} + f_{\theta}^{CNO}(\mathbf{x}_{LR})
$$

**Why used.** It gives a strong deterministic baseline and separates predictable large-scale structure from
uncertain high-frequency residuals.

**References.**
[Paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01178) ·
[Repository](https://github.com/camlab-ethz/ConvolutionalNeuralOperator) ·
[OceanLens page](cno.html)
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Flow Matching

**Role.** Generative residual model. It learns a velocity field from noise to `HR - mu`.

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = (1-t)\mathbf{x}_0 + t\mathbf{x}_1,
\qquad
\mathbf{v}^{*} = \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_0
$$

**Why used.** It samples plausible unresolved structures instead of forcing a single deterministic residual.

**References.**
[Paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747) ·
[Reference implementation](https://github.com/g4vrel/CFM) ·
[OceanLens page](flow-matching.html)
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Minibatch OT Coupling

**Role.** Pairs Gaussian noise samples with residual targets using a small optimal-transport matching inside each batch.

For a batch of noises $\{\mathbf{x}_0^i\}$ and residual targets $\{\mathbf{x}_1^j\}$, the matching solves:

$$
\pi^{*} =
\arg\min_{\pi}
\sum_{i,j} \pi_{ij}
\left\| \mathbf{x}_0^i - \mathbf{x}_1^j \right\|_2^2
$$

**Why used.** It makes the path from noise to target less arbitrary than independent random pairing, which helped
make `v4_s1_logit_t` the strongest early baseline.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Logit-normal Time Sampling

**Role.** Samples more training times around the useful middle of the path instead of treating all `t` uniformly.

Instead of $t \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$, I sample:

$$
u \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2),
\qquad
t = \mathrm{sigmoid}(u)
$$

**Why used.** The network sees more informative mixed states, where both noise and data structure are present.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Re-coarsening Consistency

**Role.** Penalizes generated HR if it does not return the LR field after downsampling.

$$
\mathcal{L}_{coarse} =
\left\| C(\widehat{\mathbf{x}}_{HR}) - \mathbf{x}_{LR}^{native} \right\|_2^2
$$

**Why used.** It is a physical consistency guardrail: the generated fine-scale field should not contradict the
coarse observation.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Classifier-Free Guidance

**Role.** Trains the model with condition dropout, then amplifies the conditional signal at inference:

$$
v_{guided} = v_{\emptyset} + w (v_{cond} - v_{\emptyset})
$$

**Why used.** It targets the under-dispersive residual problem: FM produced structures in the right places but
often with too little amplitude.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Regional Sampling

**Role.** Oversamples difficult geographic regions such as the Mediterranean, Black Sea and polar zones.

Patch candidates receive a region-dependent sampling weight:

$$
p(\text{patch}) \propto
w_{region} \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{ocean fraction} > \tau)
$$

**Why used.** Uniform patch sampling overrepresents open-ocean regions and underrepresents small enclosed seas
and ice-edge regimes.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Swin / Window Attention

**Role.** Local transformer architecture tested in `v7a`.

Self-attention is restricted to local windows:

$$
\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V)
= \mathrm{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right)V
$$

but $Q,K,V$ only interact inside a spatial window before shifted-window mixing.

**Why used.** Ocean fronts and mesoscale residuals are often local. Window attention can focus compute on nearby
structures instead of spending everything on global attention.

**Reference.** [Swin Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14030)
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Pixel DiT

**Role.** Transformer replacement for the U-Net FM backbone, tested in `v8a`.

The image is split into tokens, then each block uses attention with time/condition modulation:

$$
\mathbf{h}_{l+1}
= \mathbf{h}_{l}
+ \mathrm{Attn}(\mathrm{AdaLN}(\mathbf{h}_{l}; t, c))
$$

The conditioning $c$ contains LR/CNO/geographic information depending on the variant.

**Why used.** Tests whether global token attention and AdaLN-style conditioning help place residual energy better
than convolutional bottlenecks.

**Reference.** [Scalable Diffusion Models with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.09748)
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### EDM / SongUNet

**Role.** Diffusion-style denoising architecture tested in `v9a`.

The denoising objective trains the network to recover the clean target from a noisy state:

$$
\mathbf{y}_{\sigma} = \mathbf{y} + \sigma \boldsymbol{\epsilon},
\qquad
\mathcal{L}_{EDM}
= \lambda(\sigma)
\left\| D_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}_{\sigma}, \sigma, c) - \mathbf{y} \right\|_2^2
$$

**Why used.** CorrDiff-style downscaling uses strong diffusion backbones and stochastic samplers; this tests a
more classical score/diffusion route against FM.

**References.**
[EDM paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.00364) ·
[NVIDIA PhysicsNeMo](https://github.com/NVIDIA/physicsnemo)
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Diffusion Bridge

**Role.** Starts from a noisy CNO/LR-informed state instead of pure Gaussian noise.

Instead of a pure-noise start, the path is conditioned around the coarse estimate:

$$
\mathbf{x}_0 = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sigma(\boldsymbol{\mu})\boldsymbol{\epsilon},
\qquad
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_{HR}
$$

The learned dynamics bridge a physically informed noisy state toward HR.

**Why used.** The generative path begins closer to the physically conditioned solution, which may reduce useless
sampling variance and improve regional stability.
:::

::: {.method-entry}
### Inference Ablations

**Role.** Tests the sampler after training, without changing the weights.

For ODE-based FM inference:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = v_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_t,t,c)
$$

Euler uses one slope per step:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\Delta t\,v_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_n,t_n,c)
$$

Heun predicts once, corrects once, then averages both slopes:

$$
\mathbf{x}^{p}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\Delta t\,v_n,
\qquad
\mathbf{x}_{n+1}=\mathbf{x}_n+\frac{\Delta t}{2}(v_n+v^{p}_{n+1})
$$

**Parameters tested.**

- Solver: Euler versus Heun.
- Steps: fast `20` step runs versus heavier `25/50` step runs.
- Noise temperature: `sigma=1.0`, `1.1`, `1.2`.
- Ensemble size: `ens=1` for quick inspection, `ens=16/32` for spread.
- Tiling: `tile_size`, `tile_overlap`, and blending to reduce stitching artifacts.
- CFG scale: stronger or weaker conditioning after condition-dropout fine-tuning.

**Why used.** Some observed failures were not only training failures. Under-dispersive residuals, blue artifacts
and horizontal seams can also come from numerical integration, stochastic sampling or patch stitching.
:::
:::
